FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline data from 11,780 youth aged 9-10 years from 21 multi-site locations in the U.S.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/digital media variables: TV watching, online videos, video games, texting, social media use, video chatting (all reported separately in hours/day)

FT_IC3.evidence: Chi-square tests, logistic regression with relative risk (RR) and adjusted relative risk (ARR) comparisons; Tables 2-3 report statistical tests linking screen media to internalizing disorder diagnoses

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: β coefficients, RR, ARR with 95% CI, and p-values throughout Tables 2-3 (e.g., RR=2.00, 95% CI 1.48-2.70 for girls' depression)

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data using K-SADS-5 clinical interview with statistical hypothesis testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Affective Disorders, 2022

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (criterion c not met); screen media is independent variable predicting psychiatric outcomes as dependent variable (not vice versa per criterion a)
notes: Clear inferential statistics throughout; uses K-SADS-5 clinical interview for diagnosed disorders rather than symptom surveys

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Release 5.0, children aged 9-11 years (n=4,262) with genetically assigned European ancestry, 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Child-reported daily screen time measured from questionnaires at baseline (hours/day)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with polygenic risk scores and heritability estimates; Gsens genetic confounding framework with structural equation models reporting β coefficients

FT_IC4.evidence: Extensive numeric reporting: β coefficients with 95% CI throughout (e.g., β=0.10 SD, 95% CI 0.07-0.13 SD for screen time-attention association; β=0.03 SD, 95% CI 0.003-0.06 SD for internalizing)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using genetic sensitivity analysis model (Gsens) with SNV-based and twin-based heritability frameworks

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA Network Open, November 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Includes individual psychological/genetic predictors (ADHD PRS, depression PRS) as per criterion (b) violation; genetic factors are explicitly modeled as confounders/predictors alongside screen time
notes: Genetic confounding study; includes polygenic risk scores for ADHD and depression as individual-characteristic predictors

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study (Release 3.0) baseline and 2-year follow-up data from 5,166 participants aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) measured at baseline via parent-report and child self-report questionnaires; specific types include TV, movies, online videos, video games, texting, social media

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized linear mixed-effect models (GLMM) with mediation analyses; JIVE (Joint and Individual Variation Explained) identified brain co-development patterns; reports β coefficients, standard errors, and p-values

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric statistics reported: β=0.020 (SE=0.008, P=0.014) for SMA-internalizing association; β=0.015 (SE=0.007, P=0.029) for SMA-brain co-development; indirect effect=0.020, P=0.043

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis with mediation modeling using baseline SMA and structural brain measures to predict 2-year follow-up internalizing problems

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design includes mediation/moderation (brain co-development pattern mediates SMA-internalizing link); also reports associations with psychological outcomes (internalizing/externalizing behaviors as dependent variables, not just SMA as DV)
notes: Uses JIVE method for brain structural co-development; mediation analysis shows small but significant indirect effects through cortical-brainstem circuitry
